summary: Machine policy for verbose startup and shutdown status text. Good for troubleshooting or understanding system progress, but it makes boot, sign-in, and shutdown screens more technical.

setting.name: Verbose Status Messages
setting.casual_explanation: Windows can either show simple loading screens or more detailed status text during startup, shutdown, sign-in, and sign-out. This policy decides whether the detailed messages are shown.
setting.why_people_change_it: People enable verbose status messages when troubleshooting slow startup or shutdown behavior or when they want to see what stage Windows is in.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning it on helps explain what Windows is doing, but it also makes those system screens more technical and less clean.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents VerboseStatus through the ADMX_Logon Policy CSP and maps it to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\VerboseStatus. The public documentation describes the enabled policy state and the not-configured or disabled baseline behavior, but it does not publish a raw DWORD value table, so this record models the documented enforced state plus the default unset state.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows uses the normal simpler startup, shutdown, logon, and logoff status presentation.

allowed_values.label: Show verbose status messages
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is enabled and Windows shows detailed system-status text during startup, shutdown, sign-in, and sign-out.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes VerboseStatus = 1, which matches the documented enabled policy state.

windows_defaults.label: Windows machine-policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 10 version 2004 with KB5005101 and later, plus Windows 11 systems that expose ADMX_Logon

states.rationale: Microsoft documents the normal simpler status display when the policy is disabled or not configured.

windows_defaults.notes: The default behavior does not force verbose status text.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems where a simpler boot and sign-in experience is preferred

states.rationale: Keep the normal simpler status display.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the usual cleaner startup and shutdown screens.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need more detail about what Windows is doing during system transitions.
tradeoffs: You get less detail during troubleshooting.

recommended_profiles.label: Diagnostic visibility
avoid_for: Users who want a cleaner non-technical experience

states.rationale: Force Windows to show more detailed transition messages.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want more detail while diagnosing startup, shutdown, sign-in, or sign-out behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You do not want extra technical text during normal use.
tradeoffs: Windows shows more useful progress detail for troubleshooting.
System transition screens look more technical.

evidence.title: Microsoft ADMX_Logon Policy CSP: VerboseStatus
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents the scope, behavior, supported OS versions, and registry mapping for VerboseStatus, including the enabled policy behavior and the not-configured or disabled baseline behavior.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes VerboseStatus = 1 for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Existing tweak source record
evidence.summary: The repo already tracks provenance for this tweak in the current catalog output.

decision.why: Local Microsoft Logon policy files document the exact machine-policy surface and the verbose-status behavior. The app writes the documented enabled state.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: <policy name="VerboseStatus" class="Machine" ... key="Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System" valueName="VerboseStatus">; Logon.adml: This policy setting directs the system to display highly detailed status messages.
validation_proof.notes: Local official ADMX defines the exact machine-policy key and value name; local ADML confirms that the policy enables highly detailed status messages during system transitions.
